title: Fast convergence of Dynamic Capacities of GNS-Symmetric Quantum Channels
authors: Omar Fawzi, Li Gao, Mizanur Rahaman, Mostafa Taheri
abstract: We consider a quantum system described by a quantum channel $Φ$ that is applied at every time step and study the time evolution of its information capacities. When $Φ$ is a GNS-symmetric channel (this includes Pauli channels, for example), we give explicit exponential convergence bounds for the classical and quantum capacities. These bounds are in terms of entropic properties of $Φ$. We further illustrate how these results help quantify the performance of active versus passive error-correction setups.
summary: This work rigorously analyzes how the information capacities of quantum systems decay over time when subjected to noise modeled by a quantum channel. By focusing on GNS-symmetric channels, the authors derive explicit exponential convergence bounds for both classical and quantum capacities. Crucially, they use these bounds to demonstrate the precise crossover point where active error correction strategies provide a provable advantage over passive ones.
main_problem: The paper studies the time evolution and convergence rate of information capacities (classical and quantum) for quantum systems subjected to repeated noise modeled by a quantum channel.
main_result: For GNS-symmetric channels, the one-shot and asymptotic classical and quantum capacities converge exponentially fast to their infinite-time limits. Furthermore, the analysis quantifies the advantage of active error correction over passive methods.
method: The analysis employs functional inequalities, quantum relative entropy bounds, and techniques involving the peripheral projection of the quantum channel to derive explicit exponential convergence bounds.
model_or_system: The system is a finite-dimensional quantum memory subjected to noise described by a quantum channel $\Phi$ applied at discrete time steps, or continuous-time quantum Markov semigroups $\{T_t\}$. The focus is on GNS-symmetric channels.
key_observables: One-shot $\delta$-error classical/quantum capacities ($C_\delta, Q_\delta$), asymptotic capacities ($C, Q$), and the convergence rate quantified by exponential decay terms.
important_parameters: The convergence rate depends on entropic properties like the entropy contraction coefficient ($\alpha_c$) and the Pimsner–Popa constant ($\Lambda_c(P)$).
main_assumptions: The primary results are restricted to quantum channels exhibiting GNS-symmetry with respect to a full-rank invariant state $\sigma$.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the passive upper bound on quantum capacity (derived from Theorem 3.2) against active lower bounds (using 5-active and 25-active codes) for Pauli noise, showing a crossover time where active correction becomes superior.
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the convergence of capacities to the peripheral projection, deriving explicit exponential bounds for one-shot and asymptotic capacities, and finally applying these bounds to compare active versus passive error correction strategies.
why_interesting: It provides rigorous, quantitative bounds on how fast quantum information degrades under noise and precisely determines the threshold where active quantum error correction techniques outperform passive noise mitigation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Passive and active bounds on the quantum capacity of the iterated Pauli channel with ⃗p = (0.9986, 0.00047, 0.00047, 0.00047). The passive upper bound is obtained from Theorem 3.2; the passive lower bound is the hashing bound applied directly to T t ⃗p ; the 5-active and 25-active lower bounds are obtained by applying the hashing bound to the effective logical channels of the 5-qubit code and its recursive concatenation, rescaled by 1/5 and 1/25 respectively.
